Article.ArticleTitle: Prediction Model of Prolonged Length of Stay in Orthognathic Surgery: A Retrospective Cohort Study Under ERAS Pathways.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study aimed to identify the factors influencing prolonged hospital stay in patients undergoing orthognathic surgery (OS) and to construct a predictive model aligned with the Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocol. The goal was to facilitate the development of personalized rehabilitation plans and optimize medical resource allocation. This was a retrospective study. Clinical data from OS patients treated between July 2018 and December 2023 were retrospectively reviewed. Demographic characteristics, surgical details, and postoperative recovery parameters were analyzed. The data set was randomly partitioned into a training set and a validation set at a 7:3 ratio. Potential predictors were first screened using univariate analysis, and those with statistical significance were subsequently included in a multivariate logistic regression analysis to construct the predictive model. The model's discriminative ability was assessed using the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve, while its calibration was evaluated with the Hosmer-Lemeshow test. Independent predictive factors for prolonged hospital stay in OS patients included age, surgical duration, time to first oral intake, time to first ambulation, and other factors (n=7). The area under the ROC curve (AUC) was 0.790, corresponding to a sensitivity of 60.9%, a specificity of 84.7%, and a Youden index of 0.456, indicating a fair to good predictive ability. The Hosmer-Lemeshow test indicated good calibration (P=0.215). The predictive model established in this study demonstrated fair to good predictive power for prolonged hospital stay following OS. It shows potential in identifying high-risk patients early in the postoperative period, enabling targeted interventions.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: The authors report no conflicts of interest.
